mnemonics.title: DECENTRALIZATION Pillars
mnemonics.content:
D - Distributed Ledger
E - Economic Incentives
C - Cryptographic Security
E - Eliminated Middlemen
N - Network Consensus
T - Trustless Environment
R - Resilient Architecture
A - Autonomous Operation
L - Ledger Immutability
I - Independent Validation
Z - Zero Knowledge Proofs
A - Audit Trail
T - Transparent Operations

mnemonics.title: BLOCKCHAIN TRILEMMA
mnemonics.content:
Pick ANY Two: D-SEC-SCA
D - Decentralization
SEC - Security
SCA - Scalability

mnemonics.title: CONSENSUS Types
mnemonics.content:
PoW: Work Hard (Mining)
PoS: Stake Money (Locking coins)
DPoS: Delegate Stake (Vote for validators)
PBFT: Vote-based (Permissioned)

acronyms.usage: Consensus mechanism requiring computational work to add blocks
Consensus mechanism requiring validators to lock cryptocurrency as stake
PoS variant where token holders vote for validators
Consensus algorithms tolerating malicious nodes (up to 1/3)
Light node verification using Merkle proofs
Organization governed by smart contracts without central authority
Permissioned blockchain consensus with known validators
Runtime environment for smart contracts on Ethereum

visualTips.visualization: Think of each block as a page in a distributed ledger book. Blocks are chained together like paper clips - each block 'clips' to the previous one through its hash. Tamper with one page, and all subsequent clips break.

Imagine a family tree bottom-up: individual transactions are children, pairs combine into parents, until you reach one root (Merkle Root). To prove a transaction exists, you only need the path to root, not the entire tree.

Picture a voting system where the attacker has more than half the votes. They can always win any vote, rewrite history, and exclude others' votes. The network 'votes' with computational power (hash rate).
Soft fork = updating software while staying on the same road. Hard fork = the road splits into two separate paths. Everyone must choose which path to follow.
Imagine a busy highway (blockchain). Instead of one lane for all traffic, you create multiple parallel lanes (shards). Each lane processes its own traffic independently, multiplying capacity.

analogies.analogy: Centralized: One person holding all the answers in a classroom. Decentralized: Everyone has a textbook and can verify answers independently - no single point of failure if the teacher is absent.

Like deciding where to eat with friends: PoW is everyone racing to suggest a restaurant (winner gets to choose); PoS is whoever has the most 'influence points' (stake) gets to propose; DPoS is electing a few representatives to decide.
Like carving messages in stone. Once carved, changing it requires destroying and recreating all subsequent stones in the chain - exponentially expensive and obvious.

Full nodes are like having the entire encyclopedia stored. Light nodes are like having just the index and asking the library to verify specific facts - efficient but trusting the library's verification.
Layer 1 is the main courthouse with slow, expensive trials. Layer 2 is like parties settling disputes privately, only going to court for final judgment - much faster, still legally binding.
